content.text: Playwright and Selenium are the two dominant browser automation frameworks used to drive web browsers programmatically -- making them the foundation of both AI browser agents and automated test suites. Playwright, developed by Microsoft, communicates with browsers directly over the Chrome DevTools Protocol and ships with built-in async support, auto-waiting, and ARIA-first locators. Selenium, the older and more widely deployed framework, communicates via the W3C WebDriver protocol and supports the broadest range of browsers and language bindings. Understanding when and how to use each -- and how they differ architecturally -- is essential for anyone building reliable browser-based AI agents.

Playwright is an end-to-end browser automation library created by Microsoft in 2020. It controls Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit through a single API, with every language binding communicating via the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) for Chromium and equivalent low-level protocols for other browsers. Its headline features are auto-waiting (actions block until the target element is actionable), async-first design, isolated BrowserContexts, and network request interception.

Selenium is the original open-source browser automation framework, first released in 2004. It uses the W3C WebDriver protocol: each browser requires a separate driver executable (ChromeDriver, GeckoDriver, SafariDriver) that acts as a bridge between the Selenium client library and the browser. Selenium 4 added the Selenium Manager for automatic driver management and introduced partial support for the emerging WebDriver BiDi protocol, which bridges it toward CDP-like capabilities.

Browser automation is the execution layer for AI web agents. When an LLM decides to 'click the Submit button' or 'fill in the email field', it is Playwright or Selenium that actually drives the browser to do it. The choice of framework affects the reliability of those actions (auto-waiting vs manual waits), the debugging experience (traces, screenshots, video), and the ecosystem of integrations (test frameworks, cloud providers, AI-agent libraries).

Selenium's age means it has the deepest ecosystem: Selenium Grid for distributed test execution, extensive cloud browser provider support (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs), and tight integration with test frameworks like pytest-selenium, JUnit, and TestNG. Playwright is newer but has moved faster in providing modern capabilities: network interception, fine-grained tracing, component testing, and the Playwright MCP server for direct LLM integration.
Cross-browser automation -- both frameworks support Chrome/Edge, Firefox, and Safari, enabling cross-browser compatibility testing and agent deployment.
Headless operation -- both run headlessly in CI environments and cloud containers without a display server.
Full page interactivity -- unlike HTTP-only scrapers, both execute JavaScript, wait for AJAX calls, and interact with dynamic SPAs.
Rich debugging -- Playwright offers built-in tracing (timeline of every action with screenshots), video recording, and the Playwright Inspector debugger.
Network interception -- Playwright's page.route() lets agents mock, abort, or inspect HTTP requests; Selenium 4 adds similar capability via CDP.
Multiple contexts -- Playwright's BrowserContext provides isolated sessions within a single browser process, enabling parallel agent execution.
LLM integration -- the Playwright MCP server exposes Playwright actions as Model Context Protocol tools, enabling direct LLM-driven browser control.
Playwright does not support Safari/WebKit on non-Apple platforms in a fully native way -- WebKit builds are maintained by the Playwright team and may lag behind Apple's releases.
Selenium's per-action round-trips over HTTP WebDriver are slower than Playwright's CDP connection, especially for sequences of many small actions.
Selenium requires managing driver executables or relying on Selenium Manager; Playwright bundles its own browser binaries automatically.
Both frameworks are blocked by sophisticated anti-bot systems (Cloudflare, PerimeterX) that fingerprint browser automation characteristics.
Playwright's auto-waiting, while generally correct, can mask bugs where an element becomes briefly visible then disappears; explicit timeout tuning is sometimes needed.
Selenium's explicit and implicit wait system is less ergonomic than Playwright's; forgetting waits on dynamic content is a common source of flaky tests.
Both frameworks consume significant memory per browser instance; running hundreds of concurrent instances requires a dedicated server or cloud browser provider.

Playwright launches a browser process and connects to it over a persistent WebSocket connection using CDP for Chromium or equivalent protocols for other browsers. Every API call -- page.goto(), locator.click(), page.evaluate() -- is serialised into a protocol message, sent over the WebSocket, executed in the browser, and the result returned synchronously (from the Python caller's perspective, since await handles the async protocol round-trip).

Selenium works differently: the client library makes HTTP requests to the WebDriver server (the browser driver executable). Each action is a separate HTTP POST to a REST endpoint. The WebDriver server translates these requests into browser-native commands. This architecture is more portable (any language that can make HTTP requests can use WebDriver) but slower because each action requires an HTTP round-trip, and the WebDriver protocol exposes a narrower subset of browser functionality than CDP.

Playwright's auto-waiting is implemented as a polling loop inside the browser: before executing an action, the framework checks that the target element is attached, visible, stable (not animating), and enabled. If any condition is not met, it retries up to the configured timeout (default 30 seconds). Selenium has no equivalent built-in mechanism; developers must explicitly add WebDriverWait with ExpectedConditions.

attrs.alt: Playwright vs Selenium architecture comparison showing CDP vs WebDriver protocol layers and supported browsers
attrs.title: Playwright vs Selenium Architecture

content.text: Playwright (left) uses a persistent CDP WebSocket connection to browsers, enabling low-latency bidirectional communication and access to deep browser internals. Selenium (right) uses HTTP POST requests to a separate WebDriver server process, which then drives the browser. Both support multiple browsers but via different protocol layers. Playwright supports Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit; Selenium adds Safari via SafariDriver but lacks the deep CDP integration.
New projects where you want modern, ergonomic async/await syntax with built-in best practices.
AI browser agents that need LLM integration -- the Playwright MCP server provides first-class LLM tool support.
Projects that require network interception, request mocking, or response modification.
Teams that want rich debugging: traces, video recording, and the Playwright Inspector.
Cross-browser testing that must cover WebKit/Safari without maintaining a macOS CI machine.
Existing large test suites already written in Selenium that would cost too much to migrate.
Teams using Java, C#, or Ruby whose ecosystem integrations are more mature for Selenium.
Distributed test execution across many browsers via Selenium Grid.
Projects that require testing on real Safari (not Playwright's WebKit build) or Internet Explorer.
Integration with cloud providers (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs) that offer extensive Selenium support.

Install with pip install playwright then run playwright install to download browser binaries. The API centres on three objects: Browser, BrowserContext, and Page. A Page is the unit of interaction; a BrowserContext provides isolation between sessions; a Browser can hold many contexts.

Install with pip install selenium. Selenium Manager (built into Selenium 4.6+) automatically downloads the correct WebDriver for the installed browser version, removing the need for manual driver management. The API centres on the WebDriver object, which provides navigate_to, find_element, and send_keys methods.

Playwright's locator API is ARIA-first: get_by_role(), get_by_label(), get_by_text() are the recommended methods. Selenium's By class provides CSS_SELECTOR, XPATH, ID, NAME, CLASS_NAME, LINK_TEXT, and PARTIAL_LINK_TEXT strategies. For AI agents, prefer semantic locators in both frameworks.

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def playwright_demo():
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        # Launch with optional slow-mo for debugging
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(headless=True, slow_mo=0)

        # Isolated context: separate cookies, localStorage, auth
        context = await browser.new_context(
            user_agent='Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36',
            viewport={'width': 1280, 'height': 720},
        )

        page = await context.new_page()

        # Navigate and wait for DOM to be ready
        await page.goto('https://example.com', wait_until='domcontentloaded')

        # ARIA-first locator -- most robust
        await page.get_by_role('link', name='Get started').click()

        # Wait for network to settle after navigation
        await page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')

        # Fill form fields by label
        await page.get_by_label('Email').fill('user@example.com')
        await page.get_by_label('Password').fill('secret')
        await page.get_by_role('button', name='Sign in').click()

        # Extract page title
        title = await page.title()
        print(f'Page title: {title}')

        # Intercept network requests
        async def log_request(request):
            if '/api/' in request.url:
                print(f'API call: {request.method} {request.url}')
        page.on('request', log_request)

        # Take a screenshot
        await page.screenshot(path='/tmp/screenshot.png', full_page=True)

        # Close context (clears session data)
        await context.close()
        await browser.close()

asyncio.run(playwright_demo())

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def run_with_tracing():
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        context = await browser.new_context()

        # Start recording a trace (timeline, screenshots, network)
        await context.tracing.start(screenshots=True, snapshots=True, sources=True)

        page = await context.new_page()
        await page.goto('https://example.com/checkout')

        await page.get_by_label('Quantity').fill('2')
        await page.get_by_role('button', name='Add to cart').click()
        await page.wait_for_selector('.cart-count', state='visible')

        cart_count = await page.locator('.cart-count').text_content()
        print(f'Cart items: {cart_count}')

        # Stop trace and save to file -- open with playwright show-trace
        await context.tracing.stop(path='/tmp/trace.zip')
        print('Trace saved to /tmp/trace.zip')
        print('View with: playwright show-trace /tmp/trace.zip')

        await browser.close()

asyncio.run(run_with_tracing())

from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By
from selenium.webdriver.support.ui import WebDriverWait
from selenium.webdriver.support import expected_conditions as EC
from selenium.webdriver.chrome.options import Options
import time

def selenium_demo():
    # Selenium Manager auto-downloads ChromeDriver
    options = Options()
    options.add_argument('--headless=new')  # headless mode
    options.add_argument('--no-sandbox')
    options.add_argument('--disable-dev-shm-usage')

    driver = webdriver.Chrome(options=options)
    wait = WebDriverWait(driver, timeout=10)

    try:
        driver.get('https://example.com/login')

        # Find by CSS selector
        email_field = wait.until(
            EC.presence_of_element_located((By.CSS_SELECTOR, 'input[type="email"]'))
        )
        email_field.clear()
        email_field.send_keys('user@example.com')

        # Find by name attribute
        password_field = driver.find_element(By.NAME, 'password')
        password_field.send_keys('secret')

        # Click by XPath
        submit_btn = driver.find_element(By.XPATH, '//button[@type="submit"]')
        submit_btn.click()

        # Wait until URL changes to dashboard
        wait.until(EC.url_contains('/dashboard'))
        print(f'Logged in: {driver.current_url}')

        # Extract element text
        heading = wait.until(
            EC.visibility_of_element_located((By.TAG_NAME, 'h1'))
        )
        print(f'Heading: {heading.text}')

    finally:
        driver.quit()

selenium_demo()
Playwright network interception for AI agent observation

import asyncio
import json
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright, Route, Request

async def intercept_api_responses():
    api_data = {}

    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        browser = await pw.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = await browser.new_page()

        # Intercept all /api/ responses and cache them
        async def capture_api(route: Route, request: Request):
            response = await route.fetch()
            body = await response.body()
            if 'application/json' in response.headers.get('content-type', ''):
                try:
                    api_data[request.url] = json.loads(body)
                except Exception:
                    pass
            await route.fulfill(response=response)

        await page.route('**/api/**', capture_api)

        await page.goto('https://example.com/dashboard')
        await page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')

        # The agent now has all API responses without DOM scraping
        for url, data in api_data.items():
            print(f'{url}: {str(data)[:100]}')

        # Also block ads and analytics to speed up page load
        async def block_trackers(route: Route, request: Request):
            if any(t in request.url for t in ['analytics', 'ads', 'tracking']):
                await route.abort()
            else:
                await route.continue_()

        await page.route('**/*', block_trackers)
        await browser.close()

asyncio.run(intercept_api_responses())

A fintech company's QA team runs a regression suite of 1,200 end-to-end tests across their React banking application. They migrated from Selenium to Playwright two years ago. The migration cut suite runtime from 4 hours to 45 minutes, primarily because Playwright's auto-waiting eliminated the hundreds of explicit sleep() and WebDriverWait calls that had been added to handle race conditions. Flakiness rate dropped from 8% to under 1%.

Their AI-powered test generation system uses the Playwright MCP server to allow Claude to navigate the application, discover new pages, and generate test cases autonomously. The LLM uses Playwright's accessibility snapshot to understand the page structure, then generates locator and assertion code in Playwright's Python API. The system has generated 200 additional test cases for edge conditions that the manual test suite missed.
Using Selenium's implicit waits -- they slow down the entire test session by applying a global timeout to every findElement call; use explicit WebDriverWait instead.
Using time.sleep() in Playwright -- Playwright's actions auto-wait; adding sleep() after an action that already waits is redundant and slow.
Locating by CSS classes generated by CSS Modules -- hashed class names like _button_1a2b3 change on every build; use data-testid or role attributes.
Not closing browser contexts in Playwright -- leaked contexts consume memory; always use async with or call context.close() in a finally block.
Using the deprecated find_element_by_* methods in Selenium 4 -- they were removed; use find_element(By.CSS_SELECTOR, ...) form.
Sharing one WebDriver instance across parallel test threads in Selenium -- WebDriver is not thread-safe; each thread needs its own driver instance.
Setting Playwright timeouts too high -- a 60-second default hides slow pages; set realistic timeouts and treat timeouts as test failures.
In Playwright, prefer the Locator API over ElementHandle -- Locators are lazy, auto-retrying references that survive DOM re-renders.
Use Playwright's page.pause() during development to open the Inspector and interact manually to discover the correct selectors.
In Selenium, always use WebDriverWait with ExpectedConditions rather than implicit waits or sleep for any dynamic content.
Keep browser context creation cheap: reuse the Browser object but create fresh contexts for each test to ensure isolation.
Capture screenshots and traces on failure in CI to make debugging reproducible without a local browser.
Pin the Playwright version and run playwright install --with-deps in CI to ensure consistent browser binaries.
For AI agents, use page.accessibility.snapshot() over full HTML for LLM context -- it is smaller and more semantically informative.

Playwright's BrowserContext model enables high concurrency with low memory overhead. A single Playwright process can manage dozens of contexts, each with its own cookies, localStorage, and network state, sharing a single Chromium browser process. This is significantly more memory-efficient than launching one browser process per test, which Selenium typically requires.

Playwright's async nature allows a single Python event loop to drive multiple pages concurrently with asyncio.gather(). For CPU-bound extraction work (parsing, LLM calls), pair Playwright contexts with Python's ProcessPoolExecutor to use multiple CPU cores. Network-bound work (waiting for page loads, API responses) scales well on a single event loop.

Selenium Grid distributes tests across a grid of remote browser nodes, enabling horizontal scaling for large test suites. Playwright's equivalent is running multiple playwright workers (pytest-playwright's --numprocesses flag or k6 for load testing). Cloud browser providers (BrowserStack, LambdaTest) support both frameworks with managed browser fleets.
Exposes Playwright as Model Context Protocol tools for LLM agents
Timeline debugger: every action, screenshot, and network call
Browser extension for recording and replaying Selenium scripts
Distributed browser fleet for parallel test execution
Pytest plugin for Selenium fixture management
Cloud browser farm; remote WebDriver and Playwright endpoint
Interactive REPL for locator exploration and debugging
Mobile browser and native app automation built on WebDriver

Both frameworks launch browser processes with broad OS privileges. Running them in Docker containers or sandboxed VMs limits the blast radius if a malicious page exploits a browser vulnerability. Use --no-sandbox cautiously: it removes the Chromium process sandbox, which is needed in some container environments but increases security risk.

Credential management is critical for authenticated agents. Never pass passwords or API keys as command-line arguments (visible in process lists); use environment variables or a secrets manager. Browser contexts that cache authenticated sessions to disk (storageState) must store the state files in a location with appropriate file permissions -- treat them like password files.
Always call browser.close() or driver.quit() in a finally block to prevent orphaned browser processes.
Limit the permissions requested by browser contexts (no camera, microphone) to the minimum needed for the task.
Monitor memory usage of long-running agent sessions; browser memory can grow without bound if pages are never closed.
Use version-pinned Playwright browser binaries in production to prevent unexpected behaviour from automatic browser updates.
Playwright is the recommended choice for new AI agent projects: auto-waiting, async support, ARIA-first locators, and the MCP server make it the most capable and ergonomic option.
Selenium remains the dominant choice for existing enterprise test suites and teams needing Selenium Grid or deep cloud provider integration.
Playwright communicates over CDP for low-latency bidirectional browser control; Selenium uses the HTTP WebDriver protocol with a separate driver process.
Auto-waiting in Playwright eliminates the most common source of test flakiness; Selenium requires explicit WebDriverWait with ExpectedConditions.
BrowserContexts in Playwright enable isolated, parallel sessions within a single browser process, dramatically reducing memory overhead.
Use ARIA-first locators in both frameworks: get_by_role/get_by_label (Playwright) and CSS selectors with ARIA attributes (Selenium).
Network interception in Playwright allows AI agents to observe API responses directly, which is faster and more reliable than DOM scraping for data-heavy pages.
